title: US-005: JSON output tests for all sub-commands
description:
As a developer, I want pytest tests that verify JSON output correctness for each sub-command so that regressions are caught automatically.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Tests exist for cpu --json, memory --json, disk --json
- [ ] Each test asserts the JSON is parseable and contains expected top-level keys
- [ ] Tests assert no ANSI escape codes appear in stdout
- [ ] Error path test: simulated failure produces {"error": ...} on stderr
- [ ] pytest passes

Quality Gates:
- pytest — Python test suite

title: US-004: Structured JSON error output on failure
description:
As a developer, I want errors to be emitted as JSON to stderr when --json is active so that my scripts can detect and handle failures programmatically.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] When --json is active and an error occurs, stderr receives {"error": "<message>"}
- [ ] stdout remains empty on error
- [ ] Exit code is non-zero on error
- [ ] pytest passes

Quality Gates:
- pytest — Python test suite

title: US-003: Add `--json` flag to the `disk` sub-command
description:
As a developer, I want to run `sysinfo disk --json` so that I get structured disk usage data.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] disk sub-command accepts a --json flag
- [ ] Output is valid JSON: {"disk": {"total_gb": ..., "used_gb": ..., "free_gb": ..., "percent": ...}}
- [ ] No human-readable or decorative output on stdout
- [ ] pytest passes

Quality Gates:
- pytest — Python test suite

title: US-002: Add `--json` flag to the `memory` sub-command
description:
As a developer, I want to run `sysinfo memory --json` so that I get structured memory stats for automation.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] memory sub-command accepts a --json flag
- [ ] Output is valid JSON: {"memory": {"total_gb": ..., "used_gb": ..., "percent": ...}}
- [ ] No human-readable or decorative output on stdout
- [ ] pytest passes

Quality Gates:
- pytest — Python test suite

title: US-001: Add `--json` flag to the `cpu` sub-command
description:
As a developer, I want to run `sysinfo cpu --json` so that I get structured CPU data I can pipe into other tools.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] cpu sub-command accepts a --json flag
- [ ] Output is valid JSON printed to stdout
- [ ] JSON is nested: {"cpu": {"usage_percent": ..., "cores": ..., ...}}
- [ ] No color codes, headers, or decorative characters appear in stdout
- [ ] Human-readable output is completely suppressed
- [ ] pytest passes

Quality Gates:
- pytest — Python test suite

title: PRD: JSON Output Flag
description:
Add a --json flag to each sub-command of the sysinfo CLI so that machine-readable JSON can be produced instead of the default human-readable output. When active, all decorations, colors, and non-JSON text are suppressed and a structured nested JSON object is written to stdout.

Goals:
- Allow scripting and automation by emitting pure JSON to stdout
- Support --json as a per-sub-command flag (e.g. cpu --json, mem --json, disk --json)
- Return nested, logically grouped JSON (e.g. {"cpu": {"usage_percent": 42.1, "cores": 8}})
- Suppress all color, decorations, and human-readable text when --json is active
- Return structured error JSON ({"error": "message"}) to stderr on failure
- All stories must pass pytest

Quality Gates:
- pytest — Python test suite

Functional Requirements:
- FR-1: Each sub-command (cpu, memory, disk) must accept a --json boolean flag
- FR-2: When --json is passed, the command must write only valid JSON to stdout
- FR-3: JSON structure must be nested under a top-level key matching the sub-command name
- FR-4: All ANSI color codes and decorative characters must be absent from stdout when --json is active
- FR-5: On error, {"error": "<description>"} must be written to stderr; stdout must be empty
- FR-6: Exit code must be 0 on success and non-zero on error, regardless of --json mode

title: US-001: Add colorama dependency
description:
As a developer, I want colorama added as a project dependency so that color output works cross-platform.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] colorama is listed in requirements.txt (or equivalent)
- [ ] colorama.init() is called at program startup
- [ ] pip install -r requirements.txt && python sysinfo.py runs without error
- [ ] python -m pytest passes
- [ ] flake8 passes

title: Color Output for Sysinfo CLI
description: Add colorama-based color output to sysinfo CLI: decorative headers/labels plus semantic threshold coloring on CPU/memory/disk metrics. Respects NO_COLOR env var.

title: US-001: Display OS version as a dedicated output section
description:
As a developer, I want the CLI tool to display OS name and full version details in their own section so that I can quickly see what OS environment I'm running on.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Output includes a dedicated "OS Version" section separate from other system info
- [ ] Section displays OS name and version (e.g., macOS 14.4)
- [ ] Section also displays kernel/build version (e.g., Darwin 23.4.0)
- [ ] Format matches: OS Version: macOS 14.4 (Darwin 23.4.0)
- [ ] Section appears in the CLI output when sysinfo.py is run on macOS
- [ ] python -m pytest passes

title: OS Version Output Feature
description: Add OS version information as a dedicated output section in sysinfo.py. Displays OS name, version, and kernel/build details with graceful fallback.

title: US-001: Display Python version
description:
As a developer, I want the tool to print the current Python version in plaintext so that I can quickly confirm my runtime environment.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Running `sysinfo --python-version` (or `sysinfo version`) prints the Python version string (e.g. `Python 3.11.4`) to stdout
- [ ] Output is plaintext, one line
- [ ] Exit code is 0 on success
- [ ] pytest passes
- [ ] flake8 passes

title: Developer System Insight CLI Tool
description: A Python CLI tool giving developers on-demand snapshots of local machine system metrics (CPU, memory, disk, top processes) plus current Python version. Installable via pip, with JSON output flag.

title: US-007: pip-installable package via pyproject.toml
description: Create pyproject.toml defining the sysinfo package with entry point 'sysinfo'. After pip install ., running sysinfo from terminal works. Declare all dependencies (psutil, colorama). Include README with install and usage instructions.
close_reason: pyproject.toml created with setuptools build backend, colorama+psutil deps, sysinfo=sysinfo:main entry point. README updated. pip install -e . && sysinfo verified. 67/67 tests pass, flake8 clean. PR #16.

title: US-006: Full system snapshot with --json flag
description: Wire up all metrics (Python version, CPU, memory, disk, top processes) into the default sysinfo command output. Add --json flag that outputs a single JSON object with all metric sections. Exit code 0 on success, non-zero on failure.
notes: PR opened: https://github.com/ksclark-projects/newapp/pull/18 — all 73 tests pass, flake8 clean

title: US-005: Show top processes by CPU and memory
description: Add top-processes feature to sysinfo CLI. Show top 10 processes sorted by CPU% with columns: PID, name, CPU%, MEM%. Support --top N flag to configure count (default 10). Support --json flag. Use psutil.process_iter().
close_reason: get_top_processes(n=10) added, --top N CLI flag, table output with colorize_pct for CPU% and MEM%. Also added get_cpu_cores, get_mem_details, _fmt_size for pre-existing tests. 58/58 pass, flake8 clean. PR #15.

title: US-004: Snapshot disk usage per mount point with JSON output
description: Add disk metrics to sysinfo CLI. List each mount point with used/total/free and percent used. Support --json flag returning {"disk": [{"mount": "/", "used_gb": ..., "free_gb": ..., "total_gb": ..., "percent": ...}, ...]}. Use psutil.disk_partitions() and psutil.disk_usage().
close_reason: get_disk_mounts() implemented with psutil.disk_partitions/disk_usage; --json flag adds disk JSON output; per-mount display in main() replaces single disk line. 11 new tests, 58/58 pass, flake8 clean. PR #15.

title: US-003: Snapshot memory usage with JSON output
description: Add memory metrics to sysinfo CLI. Show used/free/cached memory in human-readable MB/GB units, plus percentage used. Support --json flag returning {"memory": {"used_mb": ..., "free_mb": ..., "cached_mb": ..., "total_mb": ...}}. Use psutil.virtual_memory().
close_reason: get_mem_details() added returning {used_mb, free_mb, cached_mb, total_mb}; _fmt_size() helper added; main() shows detail breakdown; --json includes memory key. 9 new tests, 67/67 pass, flake8 clean. PR #15 updated.

title: US-002: Snapshot CPU usage with per-core and JSON output
description: Add detailed CPU metrics to sysinfo CLI. Show overall CPU% and per-core% in text output. Support --json flag returning structured data: {"cpu": {"overall": 42.3, "cores": [...]}}. Use psutil.cpu_percent(percpu=True).
close_reason: get_cpu_cores() added using psutil percpu=True; main() shows Core N: lines after CPU Usage:. 8 new tests, 38/38 pass, flake8 clean. PR #15 open.

title: US-002: Colorize section headers
description:
As a user, I want section headers (e.g., "System Info", "CPU", "Memory", "Disk") to appear bold and in a distinct color so I can quickly locate sections.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each section header is rendered bold + cyan (or equivalent distinct color)
- [ ] Non-header lines are not affected
- [ ] Output content (text) is unchanged — only ANSI codes added
- [ ] python -m pytest passes
- [ ] flake8 passes

title: US-002: Graceful fallback when OS info is unavailable
description:
As a developer, I want the CLI tool to show a friendly message when OS version cannot be retrieved so that the tool does not crash or show confusing output.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] If OS version info cannot be retrieved, output shows: OS Version: Unknown
- [ ] The tool does not raise an unhandled exception when OS detection fails
- [ ] Other sections of the output are unaffected when OS version is unavailable
- [ ] python -m pytest passes

title: US-006: Add tests for color output
description:
As a developer, I want tests that verify color codes appear (or are suppressed) correctly so regressions are caught.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Test: with NO_COLOR unset, output for a high CPU value contains a red ANSI code
- [ ] Test: with NO_COLOR set, output contains no ANSI escape sequences
- [ ] Test: section header output contains bold ANSI code
- [ ] python -m pytest passes with 0 failures
- [ ] flake8 passes

title: US-005: Respect NO_COLOR environment variable
description:
As a user running sysinfo in a pipe or CI environment, I want color suppressed when NO_COLOR is set so output is clean for machine parsing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] When NO_COLOR is set (any value), all ANSI codes are stripped from output
- [ ] When NO_COLOR is not set, color renders normally in a TTY
- [ ] A helper function color_enabled() -> bool encapsulates this logic
- [ ] Tests cover both NO_COLOR set and unset cases
- [ ] python -m pytest passes
- [ ] flake8 passes

title: US-004: Semantic threshold coloring on numeric metrics
description:
As a user, I want CPU usage, memory usage, and disk usage values to change color based on thresholds so I can immediately spot problems.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CPU %: green < 60%, yellow 60-85%, red > 85%
- [ ] Memory %: green < 70%, yellow 70-90%, red > 90%
- [ ] Disk %: green < 70%, yellow 70-90%, red > 90%
- [ ] Color is applied to the percentage value only, not the entire line
- [ ] Thresholds are defined as named constants (not magic numbers)
- [ ] python -m pytest passes
- [ ] flake8 passes
